title: Operating System
description: In this course, you will learn the core concepts of Operating Systems, starting with their role as the bridge between hardware and software. You’ll explore how processes are created, managed, and scheduled for efficient CPU utilization, and dive into synchronization techniques to handle concurrent execution while avoiding deadlocks. The course also covers memory management strategies, including allocation methods, paging, segmentation, and virtual memory, giving you a complete understanding of how modern operating systems ensure performance, reliability, and resource utilization.

modules.name: Introduction to Operating Systems
modules.description:
This module introduces the fundamentals of operating systems and computer architecture. It covers the Von Neumann Architecture, purpose and functions of an OS, and differences between OS and application software. Learners explore various types of operating systems (batch, time-sharing, real-time) and key OS structures (monolithic, layered, modular).

The module also explains core components like the kernel, shell, file system, device drivers, system calls, and OS services. It concludes with an overview of Unix/Linux operating systems.

problems.name: Case Study - An Overview of the Linux OS

modules.name: Process Management and CPU Scheduling
modules.description:
This module covers the basics of processes and threads, including their lifecycle, structure (PCB), and operations. It introduces multithreading, thread types, and communication methods like shared memory and message passing.

It also explores CPU scheduling concepts, comparing preemptive and non-preemptive methods, and discusses key algorithms such as FCFS, SJF, Round Robin, Priority, and Multilevel scheduling. The module ends with a look at Linux process scheduling.

problems.name: Types of Thread in Operating System
Case Study - Process Scheduling in Linux

modules.name: Process Synchronization and Deadlocks
modules.description:
This module covers concurrency vs parallelism, race conditions, and the critical section problem. It introduces synchronization tools like semaphores, mutexes, and monitors, along with classic problems such as producer-consumer and dining philosophers.

It also covers deadlock concepts, including characterization, prevention, avoidance, detection, and recovery, with a focus on UNIX/Linux handling.

modules.name: Memory Management
modules.description:
This module introduces key memory management concepts, including logical vs physical address space, partitioning, and fragmentation. It covers paging and segmentation, along with related data structures like page tables, TLB, and segment tables.

The module also explains virtual memory, demand paging, and page replacement algorithms such as FIFO, LRU, and Optimal Replacement. It ends with an overview of memory management in Linux.

modules.name: File System and I/O Management
modules.description:
This module covers file system fundamentals, including file access methods, directory structures, and protection mechanisms. It explores file allocation techniques (contiguous, linked, indexed) and free space management.

It also introduces disk scheduling algorithms like FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN, and LOOK, along with the basics of I/O hardware, polling, interrupts, and DMA. The module concludes with an overview of file systems in Linux and Windows.
